equations.label: eq:spatial-equil
equations.description: Spatial equilibrium: utility $V$ is equalized across all cities $j$ via adjustment of wages $w_j$, rents $r_j$, and amenities $A_j$.

qa.question: What condition characterizes spatial equilibrium?
qa.answer: Utility V is equalized across all cities via wages, rents, and amenities

equations.label: eq:housing-supply
equations.description: Housing supply in city $j$ as a function of rents; $\varepsilon_j^S$ is the supply elasticity, which varies with physical geography.

equations.label: eq:rosen-roback
equations.latex: \frac{\partial \log r_j}{\partial A_j} = \frac{U_A}{U_r \cdot H}, \quad \frac{\partial \log w_j}{\partial A_j} = -\frac{U_A}{U_w}, \quad \hat{A}_j = \underset{A}{\operatorname{arg\,max}}\; \tilde{V}(w_j, r_j, A)
equations.description: Rosen-Roback amenity gradients: amenities are capitalized into higher rents and lower wages in equilibrium.

equations.label: eq:bid-rent
equations.latex: r(d) = r_0 - \tau d, \quad r_0 = \hat{w} - c(\tilde{H}^*), \quad r'(d) = -\tau < 0, \quad \hat{r}(d) \overset{p}{\to} r(d)
equations.description: Monocentric bid-rent gradient: rents decline with distance $d$ from the CBD at rate $\tau$ equal to commuting cost.

equations.label: eq:supply-elasticity
equations.description: Supply elasticity as a decreasing function of undevelopable land share $\text{Undev}_j$ and regulatory stringency $\text{Reg}_j$.

equations.label: eq:hedonic-price
equations.description: Hedonic house price equation: log price of house $i$ in city $j$ as a function of structural characteristics $X_{ij}$ and city fixed effect $\alpha_j$.

equations.label: eq:amenity-valuation
equations.latex: \\widetilde{A}_j = \\frac{\\partial \\log r_j / \\partial A_j}{\\partial \\log r_j / \\partial A_j - \\partial \\log w_j / \\partial A_j} \\cdot \\Delta \\log r_j + \\frac{\\partial \\log w_j / \\partial A_j}{\\partial \\log w_j / \\partial A_j - \\partial \\log r_j / \\partial A_j} \\cdot \\Delta \\log w_j
equations.description: Quality-of-life index: value of amenity $A_j$ recovered from observed rent and wage differentials using Rosen-Roback theory.

equations.label: eq:migration-gravity
equations.description: Gravity model of migration: bilateral flow $M_{ij}$ depends on distance, origin FE $\alpha_i$, destination FE $\gamma_j$, and destination real wage $\log w_j - \log r_j$.

qa.question: What is the main instrument for housing supply elasticity?
qa.answer: Undevelopable land share and terrain slope index from Saiz (2010), interacted with population growth
